subject: Radiology
front: On an X-ray, what does black versus white indicate?
back: Black = lucent; White = opaque.

subject: Radiology
front: On CT, MRI and USG, what do black versus white indicate?
back: Black = hypo (hypodense/hypointense/hypoechoic); White = hyper (hyperdense/hyperintense/hyperechoic).

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of air on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of fat on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of distilled water / a cyst on CT?
back: 0 HU

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of soft tissue on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of acute hemorrhage on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of iodinated contrast on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Hounsfield value of bone (Ca2+) on CT?

subject: Radiology
front: Does X-ray use ionising radiation?
back: Yes - X-ray uses ionising radiation.

subject: Radiology
front: On a plain X-ray, how do air and bone appear?
back: Air = black; Bone = white.

subject: Radiology
front: A chest X-ray is taken in which view, and why not the usual AP view?
back: Taken in PA view - so that the heart does not look enlarged (to reduce apparent cardiomegaly). (All other X-rays are usually taken AP.)

subject: Radiology
front: On a chest X-ray, how do the lungs differ between inspiratory and expiratory views?
back: Lungs are larger in the inspiratory view; the expiratory view is taken in the erect position. (Area of focus is the lungs.)

subject: Radiology
front: On NCCT, how do bone and air appear?
back: Bone = white; Air = black.

subject: Radiology
front: What does CECT use and what is its purpose?
back: Uses iodinated contrast - to compare vessel with muscle.

subject: Radiology
front: What are the uses of HRCT?
back: Lung parenchyma (pneumonia, Covid-19, bronchiectasis, ILD); and PNS / temporal bone.

subject: Radiology
front: On CT, what do black, white and contrast represent?
back: Black = hypodense; White = hyperdense; Contrast = iodinated (hyperdense).

subject: Radiology
front: State the relationship of CT pitch to image quality and dose.
back: Pitch is proportional to 1/quality and to 1/dose (Pitch ∝ 1/quality ∝ 1/dose).

subject: Radiology
front: How does calcification appear on CT and what is its IOC?
back: Hyperdense; IOC = intracranial calcification.

subject: Radiology
front: How do calculi appear on CT - and which calculus is the exception?
back: Calculi are hyperdense, EXCEPT gallstones (IOC = USG) - because gallstones are made of cholesterol and are not radiopaque.

subject: Radiology
front: How does bone cortex/fracture appear on CT, and which fracture is the exception?
back: Hyperdense; fracture IOC = CT, EXCEPT stress fracture (IOC = MRI) - which has no cortical discontinuity, only bone marrow edema.

subject: Radiology
front: How does acute hemorrhage appear on CT, and what are its imaging roles?
back: Hyperdense; IOC for head trauma; initial investigation in stroke.

subject: Radiology
front: How does a metallic foreign body appear on CT and what artifact does it cause?
back: Hyperdense; causes a streak artifact due to metal (whiter than the bone).

subject: Radiology
front: Causes of intracranial calcification: periventricular vs parenchymal?
back: Periventricular = CMV; Parenchymal = Toxoplasmosis.

subject: Radiology
front: Where is calcification seen in congenital Zika virus infection?
back: At the grey-white matter junction.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the IOC for osteoid osteoma?
back: NCCT.

subject: Radiology
front: On X-ray, how does an esophageal foreign body (e.g. coin) orient compared with a tracheal one?
back: Esophageal FB faces en face / coronal (round/coin-on view); tracheal FB lies sagittal (seen on its side/edge-on).

subject: Radiology
front: What X-ray sign indicates a button battery lodged in the esophagus?
back: Double ring / halo sign.

subject: Radiology
front: For a foreign body: initial investigation, IOC, and contraindicated modality?
back: Initial investigation = X-ray; IOC = CT; Contraindicated = MRI.

subject: Radiology
front: Key features of MRI (resolution, bone cortex, radiation, basis)?
back: Best resolution; bone cortex appears black; no radiation; uses nuclear magnetic resonance.

subject: Radiology
front: Briefly, how does MRI form an image from H+ ions?
back: H+ ions spin on their own axis generating magnetic energy, but have no net magnetic vector (all in opposite directions). MRI aligns these fields in one direction; when the field is removed they resonate with the outer magnetic field, forming the image.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the unit of magnetic field strength in MRI?
back: Tesla.

subject: Radiology
front: MRI is based on which property of protons?
back: Gyromagnetic property of protons.

subject: Radiology
front: What field strength is routinely used in MRI?
back: 1.5 Tesla.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the copper shielding of an MRI room called?
back: Faraday cage (continuous sheet or wire mesh of copper).

subject: Radiology
front: On T1W MRI, how do grey matter, white matter, CSF and scalp fat appear?
back: Grey matter = grey (darker); White matter = white; CSF = black; Scalp fat = white.

subject: Radiology
front: On T2W MRI, how do CSF, grey matter and scalp fat appear?
back: CSF = white; Grey matter = white; Scalp fat = white.

subject: Radiology
front: What does FLAIR stand for and how do grey/white matter and CSF appear?
back: Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery. White matter = grey; Grey matter = white; it is a T2W image but CSF is made black.

subject: Radiology
front: Why is CSF made black on FLAIR?
back: Edema in the brain is bright (white); in Multiple Sclerosis these lesions would merge with the bright CSF ventricles and be missed, so CSF is suppressed (made black).

subject: Radiology
front: What does STIR stand for and what is its principle?
back: Short Tau Inversion Recovery; it is a T2 sequence with fat suppression. On T2 both bone marrow edema and fat are hyperintense, so fat is suppressed - only edema stays white.

subject: Radiology
front: STIR is the best Ix for which conditions?
back: Bone marrow edema, stress fracture, osteomyelitis, sacroiliitis.

subject: Radiology
front: What does SWI stand for and what is it the IOC for?
back: Susceptibility Weighted Imaging; IOC for DAI (diffuse axonal injury).

subject: Radiology
front: What does DWI stand for and what are its features?
back: Diffusion Weighted Imaging; no fat, no bone, only brain is seen; most sensitive Ix to pick up stroke/ischemia; restricted diffusion appears hyperintense.

subject: Radiology
front: What does DTI stand for and how is it color coded by direction?
back: Diffusion Tensor Imaging; color coded by direction - A-P = green, R-L = red, S-I = blue. Used to see a tumor disrupting white matter.

subject: Radiology
front: IOC for tumors of CNS, spine and nerves - and the exception?
back: IOC = MRI; except during an emergency, when IOC = NCCT.

subject: Radiology
front: Which sequence is used for bone marrow edema, and in what conditions?
back: STIR sequence - for stress fracture, sacroiliitis, acute osteomyelitis.

subject: Radiology
front: Preferred modality for ligaments, cartilage and soft tissue - and is a breast implant a contraindication?
back: MRI; a breast implant is NOT a contraindication to MRI.

subject: Radiology
front: In Pancoast tumor, why is MRI the IOC (vs other lung tumors)?
back: Because the brachial plexus is affected, so IOC = MRI. For other lung tumors, IOC = CT.

subject: Radiology
front: What is time-of-flight angiography and when is it useful?
back: A non-invasive, non-contrasted enhanced MR-angiography; useful in patients with renal failure where contrast is contraindicated.

subject: Radiology
front: When is cardiac CT performed in the cardiac cycle and how?
back: In mid-diastole, assisted by synchronous ECG gating - because the least cardiac motion occurs during diastole.

subject: Radiology
front: What is cardiac CT used for in CAD?
back: Calcium scoring - denotes the degree of atherosclerosis and helps determine prognosis.

subject: Radiology
front: Difference between MDCT (multi-slice/multi-detector CT) and spiral CT per rotation?
back: MDCT takes multiple sections in 1 rotation; spiral CT takes only one section in 1 rotation.

subject: Radiology
front: What frequency range does USG use, and two general advantages?
back: 2-20 MHz; no radiation and readily available.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the USG transducer made of and on what principle does it work?
back: Lead zirconate; works on piezoelectric current.

subject: Radiology
front: Relationship between USG frequency and depth of penetration?
back: Frequency is inversely proportional to depth (frequency ∝ 1/depth).

subject: Radiology
front: Describe posterior acoustic shadowing and what produces it.
back: A stone is hyperechoic - it absorbs and refracts the waves passing through it, so it looks white. It is seen with gall stones, bones and air = 'dirty shadowing'. [Note as written: the source also labels the area behind the stone 'hyperechoic' - flagged as likely a slip, since shadowing classically casts a dark/anechoic shadow behind.]

subject: Radiology
front: Describe posterior acoustic enhancement.
back: A fluid-filled mass (cyst) lets all sound waves pass, so it is anechoic; the area behind it appears hyperechoic.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the most sensitive IOC for free fluid, and give examples of such fluid?
back: USG; e.g. cyst, ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion.

subject: Radiology
front: What do USG A-mode, B-mode and M-mode each measure?
back: A-mode = amplitude; B-mode = brightness (most commonly used); M-mode = motion.

subject: Radiology
front: What are the uses of M-mode USG?
back: Fetal heart rate (FHR) and pneumothorax.

subject: Radiology
front: On color Doppler, what do red and blue indicate, and what is this called?
back: Red = blood flowing towards the probe; Blue = blood flowing away from the probe; this is Duplex USG.

subject: Radiology
front: What does spectral Doppler show and what is it called?
back: Velocity of blood over time; called Triplex USG.

subject: Radiology
front: What determines the Doppler waveform, and what produces its three waves?
back: It depends on velocity: velocity rises in systole (wave 1) and falls in diastole (wave 2); elastic vessels in the extremities show the Windkessel effect (wave 3).

subject: Radiology
front: Where is a triphasic Doppler waveform seen?
back: In extremity arteries and arterioles.

subject: Radiology
front: Where is a biphasic Doppler waveform seen?
back: In visceral arteries.

subject: Radiology
front: Where is a monophasic Doppler waveform seen and what are its features?
back: In veins (blood returns in one direction; pulsation varies with HR and respiration) and in stenosed arteries (no change between systole and diastole); there is no flow in complete occlusion.

subject: Radiology
front: IOC for gallbladder pathology (gallstones, polyp, cholecystitis, any GB pathology)?
back: USG.

subject: Radiology
front: IOC for fluid/cyst (ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion)?
back: USG.

subject: Radiology
front: Preferred modality for pregnancy problem-solving / complex fetal imaging?
back: MRI.

subject: Radiology
front: IOC for DVT and varicose veins?
back: Doppler.

subject: Radiology
front: For arterial diseases: initial investigation, IOC and gold standard?
back: Initial = USG Doppler; IOC = CT angiography; Gold standard = DSA.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the Mickey Mouse sign on USG?
back: The great saphenous vein entering the common femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction (SFJ) - the three structures (CFA, CFV, GSV) form the Mickey Mouse appearance.

subject: Radiology
front: Which contrast agent is safe in a patient with renal failure, and why?
back: USG contrast agent ('Sonovue', given IV) - safe in renal failure because it has pulmonary excretion.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the MRI contrast agent, its route, and its main complication?
back: Gadolinium, IV route; complication = NSF (nephrogenic systemic fibrosis).

subject: Radiology
front: What is the complication of iodinated (X-ray/CT) contrast, and how is it defined and prevented?
back: Contrast-induced nephropathy - a rise in serum creatinine by 0.5 mg in 48 hours; prevention by hydration.

subject: Radiology
front: What must be ruled out before giving barium (oral/enema)?
back: Perforation, obstruction, post-op status, and TEF (tracheo-esophageal fistula).

subject: Radiology
front: In the RCM classification of contrast osmolality, which type has the highest and which the lowest osmolality?
back: Ionic (monomer/dimer) vs non-ionic (monomer/dimer): ionic monomer = highest osmolality; non-ionic dimer = lowest osmolality.

subject: Radiology
front: Which allergy should be ruled out before giving contrast?
back: Sulfa allergy.

subject: Radiology
front: Which statement is correct: HU of water, CT X-ray filtering, CT dose, or CT for gallstones?
back: The HU of water is zero (the correct statement).

subject: Radiology
front: Radiation dose of a chest X-ray (CXR)?

subject: Radiology
front: Radiation dose of a CT head?
back: 2 mSv.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiation dose of a CT thorax?
back: 5 mSv.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiation dose of a CT abdomen?
back: 5 mSv.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiation dose of a PET-CT?
back: 25 mSv.

subject: Radiology
front: A 28-year-old woman with severe headache is scheduled for CT - what must you definitely ask before the scan?
back: LMP (last menstrual period) - to rule out pregnancy before radiation exposure.

subject: Radiology
front: An imaging study of the thyroid (showing trachea, CCA, thyroid lobes) - which modality is it?
back: USG.

subject: Radiology
front: An MRI of the brain showing multiple dark microhemorrhages - which sequence is it?
back: SWI (susceptibility weighted imaging).

subject: Radiology
front: On a CXR, a structure on the right marked between the 3rd intercostal level - identified as which rib?
back: Right 4th posterior rib.

subject: Radiology
front: What percentage barium sulphate is used in a barium meal follow-through?
back: 50% barium sulphate.

subject: Radiology
front: On a barium study, how do the duodenum, jejunum and ileum appear?
back: Duodenum = smooth; Jejunum = valvulae conniventes (valves of Kerckring, feathery); Ileum = featureless.

subject: Radiology
front: Where is the tube placed in barium enteroclysis?
back: In the duodenum (advanced till the DJ flexure).

subject: Radiology
front: What does a barium enema demonstrate, and what concentration/technique is used?
back: Demonstrates haustrations; uses double contrast with 25% barium sulphate.

subject: Radiology
front: Key features of achalasia cardia on barium and its IOC?
back: Smooth tapering; LES not relaxing; it is a motility disorder; IOC = manometry; shows the bird beak sign.

subject: Radiology
front: On the Chicago classification, what defines achalasia cardia, DES and Jackhammer/Nutcracker esophagus?
back: Achalasia cardia = raised IRP + absent peristalsis [source writes 'pericarditis' - peristalsis intended]; DES = reduced distal latency; Jackhammer/Nutcracker (hypercontractile esophagus) = DCI >8000 + high-amplitude contraction.

subject: Radiology
front: Barium and staging investigations for carcinoma esophagus?
back: Irregular outline; rat tail sign; IOC = UGI endoscopy + biopsy; T/N staging = EUS; M staging = PET-CT.

subject: Radiology
front: Features and signs of diffuse esophageal spasm?
back: Reduced distal latency; diffuse contraction; corkscrew appearance; rosary bead esophagus.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of eosinophilic esophagitis?
back: Feline esophagus; transient transverse bands; involves mid-low esophagus; also seen in GERD and hiatal hernia.

subject: Radiology
front: Typical age and clinical features of intussusception?
back: Age 6-8 months; pain + bleeding with red currant jelly stool.

subject: Radiology
front: Most common type of intussusception, and its causes?
back: MC type = ileo-colic; causes = weaning and rotavirus.

subject: Radiology
front: Initial investigation and IOC/gold standard for intussusception?
back: Initial = USG; IOC/gold standard = barium enema > air enema > saline enema.

subject: Radiology
front: USG signs of intussusception?
back: Donut sign (hypoechoic cortex with hyperechoic medulla), sandwich sign, target sign, pseudo-kidney sign.

subject: Radiology
front: Barium enema sign of intussusception?
back: Coiled spring sign / claw sign.

subject: Radiology
front: Clinical features and diagnosis of pneumoperitoneum?
back: Pain abdomen + guarding/rigidity/tenderness; diagnosis = pneumoperitoneum / perforation peritonitis.

subject: Radiology
front: Next step in management of pneumoperitoneum?
back: It is a surgical emergency: IV fluids, then exploratory laparotomy.

subject: Radiology
front: Most sensitive X-ray and most sensitive investigation for pneumoperitoneum (and which contrast is contraindicated)?
back: Most sensitive X-ray = CXR PA erect; most sensitive Ix = CECT + oral contrast (gastrograffin/iohexol); barium is contraindicated.

subject: Radiology
front: What is Rigler's sign / double wall sign in pneumoperitoneum?
back: Both the inner and outer walls of the bowel/abdomen are visible.

subject: Radiology
front: On an erect CXR for pneumoperitoneum, which side is checked and why?
back: Always check the right side (for air under the diaphragm), because on the left side there is normally stomach air.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the cupola sign?
back: Air outlines the median subphrenic space.

subject: Radiology
front: Classic clinical tetrad of intestinal obstruction?
back: Pain abdomen + obstipation + abdominal distension + vomiting.

subject: Radiology
front: Most common cause of SBO vs LBO?
back: SBO = adhesions (history of prior surgery); LBO = malignancy.

subject: Radiology
front: Distribution of dilated bowel loops in SBO vs LBO?
back: SBO = central; LBO = periphery.

subject: Radiology
front: Diameter cut-offs (3-6-9 rule) for SBO, LBO and caecum?
back: Small bowel >3 cm; Large bowel >6 cm; Caecum >9 cm.

subject: Radiology
front: Valvulae conniventes, stepladder/string-of-pearls sign and haustra in SBO vs LBO?
back: Valvulae conniventes: present in SBO (jejunum), absent in LBO. Stepladder sign / string of pearls sign: + in SBO, - in LBO. Haustra: absent in SBO, ++ in LBO.

subject: Radiology
front: Management of SBO vs LBO?
back: SBO = conservative management (if no response in 24 hours, then surgery); LBO = surgery.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the string sign and in which conditions is it seen?
back: A narrowed, featureless ileum appears as a string; seen in TB (Fleischer sign on barium study), CHPS, and Crohn's disease (string sign of Kantor).

subject: Radiology
front: Barium signs of congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (CHPS)?
back: String sign, mushroom sign / Kirkin sign / umbrella sign, and single bubble sign.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the lead pipe colon and in which condition is it seen?
back: Absence of haustrations; seen in ulcerative colitis (earliest sign = mucosal granularity).

subject: Radiology
front: What is the apple core sign and what is it also known as?
back: Carcinoma colon; also known as the napkin ring sign.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the saw tooth sign, its IOC, and complication?
back: Sigmoid diverticulosis (outpouching of the sigmoid colon); IOC = colonoscopy > barium enema; complication = diverticulitis (IOC = CECT).

subject: Radiology
front: Imaging signs of hydatid cyst?
back: Honeycomb sign, water-lily sign, and calcified cyst (end stage / inactive).

subject: Radiology
front: Causative organism and most common site of hydatid cyst?
back: Echinococcus granulosus; most common site = liver > lung.

subject: Radiology
front: Name the three layers of a hydatid cyst from outer to inner.
back: Pericyst, ectocyst, and endocyst.

subject: Radiology
front: Outline the Gharbi classification of hydatid cyst (I-V).
back: I = simple cyst; II = water-lily sign / water snake type; III = honeycomb appearance; IV = complex heterogeneous type; V = calcified cyst.

subject: Radiology
front: USG features of cholelithiasis (GB stone) and the WES sign?
back: Hyperechoic stone with posterior acoustic shadow; no jaundice. WES (wall-echo-shadow) sign = a stone in a contracted gallbladder (wall, echo, shadow).

subject: Radiology
front: Features of acute cholecystitis, its IOC and most accurate test?
back: Stone impacted at the neck; thick wall >3 mm; fever, pain, tenderness; Murphy's sign positive; IOC = USG; most accurate = HIDA scan.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of a GB polyp and when to do prophylactic cholecystectomy?
back: Hyperechoic, no shadow; if >1 cm, do prophylactic cholecystectomy.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the comet tail artifact indicate in the GB, and on CT?
back: Adenomyomatosis - cholesterol crystals within Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses. Comet tail on CT (chest) = round atelectasis.

subject: Radiology
front: Management of a Phrygian cap?
back: Reassure.

subject: Radiology
front: Investigation for obstructive jaundice and the CBD cut-off indicating obstruction?
back: Initial = USG; CBD dilatation >6 mm indicates CBD obstruction (double barrel sign).

subject: Radiology
front: Why is MRCP the next/IOC for obstructive jaundice, and how does a stone appear?
back: MRCP (T2W) is non-invasive, uses no contrast and no radiation; on heavily T2W imaging all fluid is white and a stone appears as a signal void.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the gold standard for obstructive jaundice and how do stones appear on it?
back: ERCP (invasive, uses contrast and radiation); stones appear as filling defects.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of primary sclerosing cholangitis?
back: Associated with IBD; onion-skinning duct lesions; pANCA positive; MRI shows a beaded appearance.

subject: Radiology
front: Work-up of a suspected bile leak (initial, best, most sensitive)?
back: History of cholecystectomy with increased drain output/jaundice. Initial = USG (USG-guided pigtail catheter); best = ERCP (stent); most sensitive = HIDA (shows the leak but not its type or site).

subject: Radiology
front: A 6-month-old baby with bilious vomiting; abdominal X-ray shows a double bubble - diagnosis?
back: Duodenal atresia.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiological IOC for pheochromocytoma?
back: MRI.

subject: Radiology
front: An elderly lady with abdominal pain, distension and chronic constipation - most likely diagnosis?
back: Sigmoid volvulus.

subject: Radiology
front: Sigmoid vs cecal volvulus - sign, haustra and number of loops?
back: Sigmoid: coffee bean sign, haustra absent, 2 loops. Cecal: C-sign, haustra present, 1 loop (predisposing factors: pregnancy, pelvic surgery).

subject: Radiology
front: A 48-year-old lady with RUQ pain, multiple GB calculi, no wall thickening, CBD 12 mm, GGT 5x raised, ALP 400 IU - next step?
back: MRCP.

subject: Radiology
front: A 40-year-old with jaundice, raised bilirubin and GGT, scleroatrophic GB with dilated CBD and impacted calculi - next step?
back: ERCP.

subject: Radiology
front: A 60-year-old with increased bowel sounds, dilated small bowel loops and air in the biliary tree (h/o hysterectomy) - diagnosis?
back: Gallstone ileus - Rigler's triad = pneumobilia (air in biliary tree) + small bowel obstruction + stone at the ileocaecal junction.

subject: Radiology
front: A neonatal abdominal X-ray showing the football sign - diagnosis?
back: Pneumoperitoneum.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the cobra head / adder head sign indicate on IVP?
back: Ureterocele (cystic dilatation of the distal part of the ureter).

subject: Radiology
front: What does the hand-shaking calyces / flower vase sign indicate, and its key features?
back: Horseshoe kidney - the lower poles of the calyces are fused; ascent is prevented by the IMA (inferior mesenteric artery); there is an increased risk of TCC.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of putty kidney (GU TB) and its most sensitive Ix?
back: Sterile pyuria; thimble bladder; increased frequency of voiding; most sensitive Ix = IVP; moth-eaten / phantom calyces.

subject: Radiology
front: What organism, stone type and crystals are associated with a staghorn calculus?
back: Proteus infection; struvite (triple phosphate) stone; coffin-lid crystals.

subject: Radiology
front: Causes of nephrocalcinosis (bilateral medullary)?
back: Increased calcium, oxaluria, and increased PTH.

subject: Radiology
front: On which modality is the Bosniak classification done, and the malignancy risk of a simple cyst?
back: CECT; a simple cyst has 0% risk of malignancy.

subject: Radiology
front: Features and signs of AD PCKD?
back: Bilateral renal cysts; associated with berry aneurysm; shows the spider leg sign (on IVP).

subject: Radiology
front: Describe the MCU (micturating cystourethrogram) procedure.
back: 450-500 ml saline + contrast is injected into the bladder through a Foley's catheter; the person is asked to void and an X-ray is taken, opacifying the bladder and urethra.

subject: Radiology
front: Name the parts of the urethra and which form the anterior urethra.
back: Prostatic, membranous, bulbar and penile; the bulbar + penile parts form the anterior urethra.

subject: Radiology
front: What is VUR the most common cause of, and what defines grade V?
back: Most common cause of recurrent UTI in children; grade V = a dilated, tortuous ureter with ballooning of the calyces.

subject: Radiology
front: What is PUV the most common cause of, and its key feature?
back: Posterior urethral valves - the most common cause of obstructive uropathy in children; the valve does not allow contrast to pass beyond it.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the keyhole sign indicate, and what is associated with it?
back: Posterior urethral valves (PUV); associated with oligohydramnios.

subject: Radiology
front: Clinical features of urethral trauma?
back: Inability to void urine and blood at the meatus.

subject: Radiology
front: Risk factors for anterior vs posterior urethral injury?
back: Anterior = straddle injury; Posterior = pelvic fracture.

subject: Radiology
front: Which procedures are contraindicated and what is the IOC in urethral trauma?
back: Foley's catheter and MCU are contraindicated; IOC = RGU (retrograde urethrogram).

subject: Radiology
front: Management pathway for urethral trauma?
back: SPC (supra-pubic cystostomy), then after 4-6 weeks delayed urethroplasty (definitive treatment); if the urinary bladder is not palpable, wait and watch.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the IOC for bladder trauma?
back: CT cystography.

subject: Radiology
front: Sign and management of extraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Molar tooth sign / flame sign; managed conservatively.

subject: Radiology
front: Management of intraperitoneal bladder rupture?
back: Surgery.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the earliest structure seen in pregnancy and at what gestational age (TVS vs TAS)?
back: Gestational sac; TVS = 4.5-5 weeks, TAS = 5.5-6 weeks.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the earliest sign of the gestational sac and its appearance?
back: Intra-decidual sign - eccentric, with a well-defined wall. (The double decidual sign is also seen.)

subject: Radiology
front: At what gestational age is the yolk sac seen?
back: 5.5-6 weeks.

subject: Radiology
front: At what gestational age is the embryo/fetal pole seen, and what is the surest sign of viability?
back: 6.5-7 weeks; FHS positive (fetal heart sound) is the surest sign of viability.

subject: Radiology
front: What two structures make up the double bleb sign?
back: The amniotic sac and the yolk sac.

subject: Radiology
front: A 45-year-old with a renal mass on CECT; which renal tumor, and what is it associated with?
back: Renal angiomyolipoma; associated with tuberous sclerosis (features: seizures, subependymal nodules, adenoma sebaceum, shagreen patch).

subject: Radiology
front: A 55-year-old man with a renal mass; most common type, association, and features?
back: Renal cell carcinoma; most common = clear cell RCC; associated with VHL (chromosome 3p); features include hemangioblastoma (spinal, cerebellar) and pheochromocytoma.

subject: Radiology
front: A 45-year-old with abnormal uterine bleeding; USG shows a feeding vessel sign - diagnosis?
back: Endometrial polyp.

subject: Radiology
front: A 34-year-old on OCP with amenorrhea; best method to calculate gestational age and why?
back: CRL by USG - because LMP is unreliable (forgotten LMP, irregular menses, lactational amenorrhea, history of OCP use).

subject: Radiology
front: A 30-year-old with a pelvic fracture, unable to pass urine, with blood at the meatus; RGU performed - most likely site of urethral injury?
back: Membranous urethra (posterior urethra - associated with pelvic fracture).

subject: Radiology
front: An RGU showing a narrowing of the anterior urethra - identify the finding?
back: Bulbar stricture.

subject: Radiology
front: An HSG showing bilateral dilated tubes (h/o TB/PID) - diagnosis?
back: Bilateral hydrosalpinx.

subject: Radiology
front: An HSG showing a single uterine horn - diagnosis?
back: Unicornuate uterus.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiological sign and earliest finding of pleural effusion?
back: Sign = meniscus / Ellis S-curve sign; earliest finding = blunting of the costophrenic (CP) angle.

subject: Radiology
front: Direction of mediastinal shift in pleural effusion?
back: Towards the contralateral side.

subject: Radiology
front: Most sensitive investigation and most sensitive X-ray for pleural effusion (with detectable volumes)?
back: Most sensitive Ix = USG (detects 5-10 mL); most sensitive X-ray = ipsilateral lateral decubitus (detects 50 mL).

subject: Radiology
front: Direction of mediastinal shift, most sensitive Ix and most sensitive X-ray for pneumothorax?
back: Shift = contralateral side; most sensitive Ix = CT; most sensitive X-ray = expiratory CXR (makes the normal lung appear white).

subject: Radiology
front: CXR findings of pneumothorax?
back: Hyperlucent lung, absent bronchovascular markings, and a visceral pleural line of demarcation.

subject: Radiology
front: Management of an unstable (tension) pneumothorax (e.g. trauma, SBP <90)?
back: Needle thoracostomy, followed by ICD once stable.

subject: Radiology
front: Site and needle size for needle thoracostomy in tension pneumothorax?
back: 5th ICS in the mid-axillary line in adults; 2nd ICS in the mid-clavicular line in children; needle = 14 G, 3.25 inch.

subject: Radiology
front: Management of a stable pneumothorax, and the borders of the triangle of safety?
back: ICD placed in the triangle of safety; borders = pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and the rib below.

subject: Radiology
front: M-mode USG appearance of normal lung vs pneumothorax?
back: Normal lung = seashore sign; pneumothorax = barcode / stratosphere sign.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the air bronchogram sign indicate and where is it seen?
back: Consolidation (alveolar air replaced by fluid/pus); seen in pulmonary edema (h/o heart failure) and pneumonia (h/o expectoration).

subject: Radiology
front: What does the tree-in-bud sign indicate, and why is it not seen in Covid-19?
back: Active TB (endobronchial) - nodules along the bronchus; not seen in Covid-19 because that is an alveolar disease.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the signet ring sign indicate and what is the criterion?
back: Bronchiectasis - the diameter of the bronchus is greater than the adjacent pulmonary artery; also shows a tram-track appearance.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the halo sign indicate and its features?
back: Invasive aspergillosis (in an immunocompromised patient) - a solid center with a hazy periphery.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the reverse halo sign indicate?
back: Organising pneumonia and mucormycosis.

subject: Radiology
front: What does ground glass opacity suggest, and what CORADS grade is typical Covid?
back: Covid-19; typical Covid = CORADS-5.

subject: Radiology
front: Outline the CORADS grading (0-6).
back: 0 = incomplete; 1 = normal; 2,3,4 = atypical; 5 = typical Covid; 6 = RT-PCR proven.

subject: Radiology
front: Most common cause of miliary nodules, and other causes?
back: MCC = TB; other causes = histoplasmosis, varicella, TPE (tropical pulmonary eosinophilia), silicosis.

subject: Radiology
front: What does honeycombing indicate?
back: UIP (usual interstitial pneumonia) / ILD.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the silhouette sign indicate?
back: Right middle lobe consolidation.

subject: Radiology
front: A pneumatocele is associated with which pneumonia?
back: Staphylococcus aureus pneumonia.

subject: Radiology
front: Popcorn calcification on mammography suggests which lesion, and its BIRADS?
back: Fibroadenoma; calcification present = BIRADS 2, calcification absent = BIRADS 3; most sensitive Ix = MRI.

subject: Radiology
front: Popcorn calcification on CT chest suggests which lesion?
back: Pulmonary hamartoma - the most common benign tumor of the lung.

subject: Radiology
front: Direction of mediastinal shift in consolidation, pleural effusion and lung collapse?
back: Consolidation = no shift; pleural effusion (e.g. massive) = contralateral shift; lung collapse = ipsilateral shift.

subject: Radiology
front: A 28-year-old with HIV, CD4 120, and non-productive cough; B/L GGOs + cysts - diagnosis?
back: Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) pneumonia.

subject: Radiology
front: A 55-year-old smoker with weight loss and a cannon-ball appearance on CXR - diagnosis?
back: Metastasis.

subject: Radiology
front: A CXR with mediastinal shift to the contralateral side - which finding?
back: Pleural effusion.

subject: Radiology
front: A CT showing an aberrant right subclavian artery causes which symptom?
back: Dysphagia lusoria.

subject: Radiology
front: Egg-shell calcification is seen in all of the following EXCEPT - silicosis, sarcoidosis, post-radiation LNs, metastatic LNs?
back: Metastatic lymph nodes are the exception (egg-shell calcification IS seen in silicosis, sarcoidosis and post-radiation therapy lymph nodes).

subject: Radiology
front: In blunt chest trauma, which CXR findings need further evaluation before a chest tube - pneumothorax, hemothorax, RML consolidation, diaphragm injury?
back: RML consolidation and diaphragm injury (these are not pneumothorax/hemothorax requiring a tube).

subject: Radiology
front: A 25-year-old with mild intermittent upper abdominal pain; CXR shows a retrocardiac gas shadow - diagnosis?
back: Hiatal hernia.

subject: Radiology
front: A cattle handler with cough, hemoptysis and chest pain; CXR shown - diagnosis?
back: Hydatid cyst.

subject: Radiology
front: A 35-year-old with Raynaud's, skin tightening (scleroderma), dyspnea and bilateral basal rales - next best step?
back: HRCT chest.

subject: Radiology
front: A 54-year-old with dyspnea, eosinophil count 5000, and miliary mottling on CXR - diagnosis?
back: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the IOC for head trauma?
back: NCCT.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of subdural hematoma (SDH) - vessel, trauma, suture/midline crossing?
back: Bridging veins; trivial trauma; crosses suture lines but does NOT cross the midline (crescent-shaped); classic in alcoholics with a fall.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of epidural hematoma (EDH) - vessel, trauma, suture/midline crossing?
back: Artery; due to RTA; does NOT cross suture lines but DOES cross the midline (biconvex). Lucid interval is more typical of EDH than SDH.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) - presentation, cause, MC site, IOC, gold standard?
back: Thunderclap headache; trauma > aneurysm (berry); MC site = ACA-ACom junction; IOC = CT angiography; gold standard = DSA.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of diffuse axonal injury (DAI) - NCCT finding and IOC?
back: NCCT is normal or shows petechial hemorrhages; IOC = SWI.

subject: Radiology
front: Most common site, most common risk factor, and vessel involved in ICH?
back: MC site = putamen; MC risk factor = hypertension; vessel = artery of Charcot (a branch of the MCA).

subject: Radiology
front: What does the dural tail sign indicate, and a histology clue?
back: Meningioma (the most common benign brain tumor); psammoma bodies.

subject: Radiology
front: What does a butterfly tumor indicate, and a histology clue?
back: Glioblastoma (the most common malignant brain tumor); pseudopalisading serpentine necrosis.

subject: Radiology
front: What does the ice cream cone sign indicate?
back: Acoustic neuroma / vestibular schwannoma (the most common cerebellopontine angle tumor).

subject: Radiology
front: Pilocytic astrocytoma - significance and histology clue?
back: The most common benign brain tumor in children; Rosenthal fibres.

subject: Radiology
front: Medulloblastoma - significance, histology clue, and spread?
back: The most common malignant brain tumor in children; Homer-Wright pseudo-rosettes; drop metastases.

subject: Radiology
front: Craniopharyngioma - location, effect, and key features?
back: Supra-sellar - compresses the optic chiasma; shows calcification, occurs in children, and is cystic; H/P = machine oil in the cyst.

subject: Radiology
front: Imaging features of a cerebral abscess?
back: Diffusion restriction and ring enhancement.

subject: Radiology
front: Features of neurocysticercosis?
back: Caused by T. solium eggs; cyst with a central scolex; starry sky appearance.

subject: Radiology
front: Imaging features of CNS tuberculosis?
back: Conglomeration; lipid-lactate peak (on MR spectroscopy).

subject: Radiology
front: Features of cerebral toxoplasmosis?
back: Seen in HIV; presents as a child with abnormal behaviour.

subject: Radiology
front: What is best seen on Pierre's (Water's) view, and how is the sphenoid sinus seen?
back: Maxillary sinus is seen best; with the mouth closed it is the Water's view; the mouth is opened to see the sphenoid sinus; the posterior ethmoidal sinus is not visible.

subject: Radiology
front: What is Caldwell's view best for, and what obscures the maxillary sinus on it?
back: Best for the frontal sinus; the pterygoid plates obscure the maxillary sinus.

subject: Radiology
front: The Mt. Fuji sign is seen in which condition?
back: Pneumocephalus.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the management of choice for aneurysms performed by interventional radiologists?
back: Coiling.

subject: Radiology
front: The banana sign on USG is associated with which condition?
back: Spina bifida.

subject: Radiology
front: Which two fetal cranial USG signs are seen in spina bifida (neural tube defect)?
back: The lemon sign and the banana sign.

subject: Radiology
front: Increased serum alkaline phosphatase with cotton wool spots on skull X-ray - diagnosis?
back: Paget's disease.

subject: Radiology
front: A structure marked in a CT of the paranasal sinuses (with concha bullosa noted) - identified as?
back: Anterior ethmoidal canal.

subject: Radiology
front: Which arterial territory is involved in an infarct shown on CT between the ACA and PCA territories?
back: MCA.

subject: Radiology
front: A 15-year-old male with epistaxis on CECT - which sign, and what does it indicate?
back: Homan-Miller sign - indicates JNA (juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma).

subject: Radiology
front: Match the signs: Dodd, Phelp, Griesinger?
back: Dodd sign = polyp; Phelp sign = glomus; Griesinger sign = lateral sinus thrombosis.

subject: Radiology
front: Chondroblastoma (Codman's tumor) - age, location, histology?
back: Age <20 years; epiphyseal tumor; HPE = chicken-wire calcification.

subject: Radiology
front: GCT / osteoclastoma - age, location, appearance and cells?
back: Age 30-40 years; epiphyseal tumor; soap-bubble appearance (giant cells + mononuclear cells).

subject: Radiology
front: Treatment of chondroblastoma and GCT?
back: External curettage + grafting.

subject: Radiology
front: Osteoid osteoma - location, clinical feature, IOC and treatment?
back: Diaphyseal tumor; nocturnal pain relieved by NSAIDs (the lucent nidus releases PGE2); IOC = CT; treatment = radiofrequency ablation.

subject: Radiology
front: Ewing sarcoma - radiosensitivity, treatment, age, periosteal reaction, histology, genetics?
back: Most radiosensitive (treated with RT + CT); diaphyseal; onion-peel periosteal reaction; age <20 years; HPE = Homer-Wright pseudorosettes; t(11,22).

subject: Radiology
front: Adamantinoma - location?
back: A diaphyseal tumor, classically in the tibia.

subject: Radiology
front: Osteosarcoma - age, location, radiosensitivity, treatment, periosteal signs?
back: Age <20 years; metaphyseal; radioresistant (Rx = surgery); is an RT-induced tumor; shows Codman's triangle and sunburst periosteal reaction.

subject: Radiology
front: Simple bone cyst (SBC) - location, structure, signs, treatment?
back: Metaphyseal tumor; a single unicameral cavity; fallen leaf sign and trapdoor sign; Rx = simple curettage.

subject: Radiology
front: Aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC) - location, appearance, treatment?
back: Metaphyseal tumor; soap-bubble appearance; Rx = external curettage + grafting.

subject: Radiology
front: What cartilage cap thickness on MRI suggests malignant transformation of an osteochondroma?
back: A cap >1.5 cm.

subject: Radiology
front: Enchondroma - distribution and the two associated syndromes?
back: Frequent and multiple, involving short bones; Ollier's syndrome = multiple enchondromas only; Maffucci syndrome = enchondromas + hemangiomas. Rx = external curettage + grafting.

subject: Radiology
front: Fibrous dysplasia - deformity, histology, and the associated syndrome?
back: Shepherd's crook deformity; HPE = Chinese-letter arrangement; polyostotic involvement with precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait spots = McCune-Albright syndrome.

subject: Radiology
front: Rugger-jersey spine is seen in which conditions?
back: Secondary hyperparathyroidism / renal osteodystrophy.

subject: Radiology
front: Ivory vertebra is seen in which conditions?
back: Paget's disease (blastic phase), blastic metastases (breast, prostate), Hodgkin lymphoma, and carcinoid syndrome.

subject: Radiology
front: Codfish vertebra indicates which condition, its IOC and diagnostic cut-off?
back: Osteoporosis; IOC = DEXA; T-score <= -2.5 SD (compared to a young adult).

subject: Radiology
front: Ankylosing spondylitis - patient profile, HLA, signs and extra-articular features?
back: Young male with inflammatory backache; HLA-B27 positive; signs = dagger sign / tram-track / bamboo spine; uveitis and bilateral heel pain.

subject: Radiology
front: Scoliosis - what is measured and which clinical test is used?
back: Cobb's angle is measured; Adam's forward bending test is used.

subject: Radiology
front: A knee X-ray showing a transverse lucency through the patella - diagnosis?
back: Patella fracture.

subject: Radiology
front: A 5-year-old boy with inability to pronate and supinate the forearm - diagnosis?
back: Radioulnar synostosis.

subject: Radiology
front: A 50-year-old male with diffuse back pain; cervical spine X-ray with flowing ossification - diagnosis?
back: DISH (diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis).

subject: Radiology
front: A 70-year-old with a painful limp, no drug/trauma history; hip X-ray shows a crescent sign - diagnosis?
back: AVN (avascular necrosis).

subject: Radiology
front: A marked posterior knee ligament on MRI - which ligament?
back: PCL (posterior cruciate ligament).

subject: Radiology
front: A 40-year-old with progressive forward bending and increasing spinal stiffness; CT-spine shown - diagnosis?
back: Ankylosing spondylitis.

subject: Radiology
front: On PET-CT, what do the yellow and white represent, and what tracer is used?
back: Yellow = PET signal, white = bone; tracer = 18-FDG (deoxyglucose), which emits a positron and is taken up by malignant tissue.

subject: Radiology
front: Causes of a false-positive PET, and the physiological hotspots?
back: False positives = carcinoid and mucinous adenocarcinoma; physiological hotspots = brain and bladder.

subject: Radiology
front: What is a TLD badge, what is it made of, where is it worn, and how often checked?
back: A thermoluminescent dosimeter - a personal dosimeter to check radiation exposure; made of LiF/CaSO4; worn below the lead apron at chest level; checked 3-monthly.

subject: Radiology
front: What thickness of lead apron is most commonly used and the minimum thickness?
back: Most commonly used = 0.5 mm; minimum = 0.25 mm.

subject: Radiology
front: How does a LINAC (linear accelerator) work?
back: It accelerates electrons and allows them to collide with a heavy metal target to produce high-energy X-rays.

subject: Radiology
front: What is the principle of brachytherapy and its uses?
back: Remote afterloading; uses = prostate CA, CA cervix, eyelid.

subject: Radiology
front: What is gamma knife therapy, what source does it use, and its uses?
back: Stereotactic radiosurgery - a targeted attack without damage to surrounding structures; uses Co-60; good for inoperable brain tumors - solitary mets, pituitary adenoma, vestibular schwannoma, refractory trigeminal neuralgia.

subject: Radiology
front: What particle does a cyclotron produce, and what is the Bragg peak used for?
back: Cyclotron produces protons; the Bragg peak (protons) is used for deep-seated tumors (e.g. clival chordoma), while electrons are used for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and intra-op RT.

subject: Radiology
front: By Bergonie's law, which cells are most vs least radiosensitive?
back: Most sensitive = actively dividing cells; least sensitive = quiescent cells.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive organ?
back: Most = gonads (ovary > testis); least = vagina.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive blood cell?
back: Most = lymphocyte; least = platelet > RBC.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive cell cycle phase?
back: Most = G2-M; least = S phase.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive tissue?
back: Most = hematopoietic / bone marrow; least = CNS.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive structure of the eye?
back: Most = lens; least = sclera.

subject: Radiology
front: Most vs least radiosensitive tumor?
back: Most = Ewing's sarcoma; least = osteosarcoma.

subject: Radiology
front: Sequence and dose ranges of the acute radiation syndrome?
back: Hematopoietic (1-2 Gy) -> GI system (10-20 Gy) -> CVS-CNS (20-100 Gy).

subject: Radiology
front: State the inverse square law for radiation intensity.
back: Intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance (I proportional to 1/d^2).

subject: Radiology
front: List the radiosensitive tumors.
back: Wilm's tumor, Ewing's sarcoma, Lymphoma/Leukemia, Multiple myeloma, Seminoma. (Mnemonic: WELMS.)

subject: Radiology
front: List the radioresistant tumors.
back: HCC/RCC, Osteosarcoma, Melanoma, Pancreatic CA. (Mnemonic: HOMP.)

subject: Radiology
front: How does metastasis vs a superscan appear on a bone scan?
back: Metastasis = multiple discrete hot spots; superscan = diffuse increased skeletal uptake with reduced renal and soft-tissue activity.

subject: Radiology
front: Which malignancy classically shows cold spots on a bone scan?
back: Multiple myeloma.

subject: Radiology
front: A Ga-68 PSMA scan is used to diagnose which condition?
back: Prostate cancer.

subject: Radiology
front: A linear accelerator is used for the production of what?
back: X-rays.

subject: Radiology
front: Which statement about radiotherapy is true (re: dose and distance)?
back: The dose of radiation at a point is inversely proportional to the square of its distance from the source.

subject: Radiology
front: Why does a prostate cancer bone scan show a superscan appearance?
back: Increased uptake due to diffuse metastasis.

subject: Radiology
front: Radiotherapy given as 1.8-2 Gy daily, 5 days/week, over 6-8 weeks is which type of fractionation?
back: Conventional fractionation radiotherapy.

subject: Radiology
front: Most sensitive investigation for parathyroid tumor imaging?
back: Tc-Sestamibi SPECT (single photon emission CT).
